mnemonics.title: FIOPS - File IO Operations
mnemonics.content: FIOPS stands for: Find file → Open file → Input/Output data → Process data → Shut (close) file. This is the sequence of steps for any file operation in C++.

mnemonics.title: iFF OOO - ifstream For Fetching, ofstream For Outputting
mnemonics.content: iFF (ifstream) = Input File ForFetching data FROM file TO program. OOO (ofstream) = Output File For Outputting data FROM program TO file.

mnemonics.title: R-WAP - Read Write Append Position
mnemonics.content: Remember file modes: R (ios::in - Read), W (ios::out - Write), A (ios::app - Append), P (ios::ate - Position at end initially, but allows movement).

mnemonics.title: GPS - Get Put Seek
mnemonics.content: G = seekg() moves GET pointer, P = seekp() moves PUT pointer, S = both Seek functions need offset and reference position (beg, cur, end).

mnemonics.title: BIRD - Binary Read
mnemonics.content: B = buffer for read(), I = integer size (sizeof), R = read(bytes), D = destination char* buffer. Binary read/write uses char* buffers.

acronyms.usage: Use when you need to READ data from a file into your program. Opens file in read mode by default.
Use when you need to WRITE data from program to a file. Creates new file or overwrites existing by default.
Use when you need BOTH reading and writing on the same file. Requires specifying mode explicitly.
Moves the reading position in the file. Syntax: seekg(offset, reference_position). Reference: ios::beg, ios::cur, ios::end.
Moves the writing position in the file. Syntax: seekp(offset, reference_position). Used before write() operations.
Returns current position of the GET (read) pointer as streampos. Returns -1 if no file open.
Returns current position of the PUT (write) pointer as streampos. Used to track where next write will occur.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a file as a long railway track. The file pointers (get/put) are like trains moving along the track. seekg() and seekp() are like choosing which station (byte position) the train should go to. tellg() and tellp() are like checking where the train is currently located.

Think of ifstream, ofstream, and fstream as three floors of a building. Ground floor (ifstream) = input/reading. Top floor (ofstream) = output/writing. Middle floor (fstream) = both operations. When you open a file, you're 'unlocking' one of these floors.

Picture a dial with file modes: ios::in (read), ios::out (write), ios::app (append), ios::trunc (truncate), ios::binary (raw bytes). Each mode is like a different setting on a camera - choose the right setting for what you want to capture.

visualTips.concept: Bucket and Spade for Binary I/O
visualTips.visualization: For binary read()/write(), imagine using a bucket (char* buffer) to scoop up exact amounts of water (bytes) from a pond (file). You need to know the bucket size (number of bytes) before you scoop.

analogies.analogy: It's like borrowing a book from a library. You need to request (open) the file before reading (ifstream) or writing (ofstream). When done, you must return it (close) - otherwise you might lose changes or block others from using it.

Think of a diary: ios::out is like tearing out all pages and starting fresh each time. ios::app is like adding new pages to the end without disturbing what's already written. ios::in is like reading your old entries.

File pointers are like bookmarks in a notebook. When you read, you move the 'read' bookmark. When you write, you move the 'write' bookmark. seekg() moves the read bookmark, seekp() moves the write bookmark. They can be at different places in the same file.

Text mode is like reading a book with automatic translation (handles 
 to 
 conversion). Binary mode is like reading raw code - no translation, you get exactly what's stored. Use text for documents, use binary for images/structures.

fstream is like a read-write notebook. You can read from any page and write on any page. But unlike ofstream (which writes from the beginning), you control where you write using seekp(). And unlike ifstream (which only reads), you can also add new content anywhere.
